The company develops AI-powered tools that allow enterprises to generate everything from product imagery and advertising assets to gaming characters and film visuals while maintaining brand consistency and legal compliance. According to Vered, who leads AI strategy at Bria, the company was founded on the belief that enterprises need more than general-purpose AI models to meet professional content creation standards. “Marketing and media teams had two bad options,” Vered said during the interview. “They could either create content manually, which was safe and on-brand but slow, or they could use generic AI, which was fast but legally risky and often off-brand.” Bria’s platform aims to bridge that gap by delivering what the company describes as the best of both worlds: the speed and efficiency of AI combined with enterprise-grade safety, transparency and brand control. The company trains its proprietary text-to-image and text-to-video foundation models exclusively on licensed content and provides attribution back to original creators, creating what it calls a sustainable ecosystem for AI-generated media. AI Product.content: [Latest News](/news) [## OpenAI models running benchmark breached AI platform Hugging Face](/news/openai-models-running-benchmark-breached-ai-platform-hugging-face-627560) [## MMG deploys digital twin at Queensland zinc mine](/news/mmg-deploys-digital-twin-at-queensland-zinc-mine-627558) [## CommBank iQ is putting AI to work on its core dataset](/news/commbank-iq-is-putting-ai-to-work-on-its-core-dataset-627505) [## ACMA pledge scheme helps decimate dodgy device listings](/news/acma-pledge-scheme-helps-decimate-dodgy-device-listings-627541) [## State of Data & AI 2026](/state-of-data-ai-2026) * [Home](https://www.itnews.com.au) * [News](https://www.itnews.com.au/news) * [Technology](https://www.itnews.com.au/technology) * [Software](https://www.itnews.com.au/technology/software) # OpenAI models running benchmark breached AI platform Hugging Face By [Juha Saarinen](/author/juha-saarinen-1962724) Jul 22 2026 11:39AM ## Guardrails for commercial AIs prompted analysts to switch to Chinese model. Artificial intelligence company OpenAI has confirmed that the mystery attacker behind last week's Hugging Face breach was two of its own models running loose during an internal test to obtain a benchmark score. OpenAI [said](https://openai.com/index/hugging-face-model-evaluation-security-incident/) that GPT-5.6 Sol and an unnamed, "even more capable pre-release model" breached the infrastructure of AI platform provider Hugging Face while undergoing an internal cyber security benchmark evaluation by the vendor. Both models were running with reduced cyber refusals for an internal evaluation against [ExploitGym](https://www.cybergym.io/exploitgym/), a benchmark of real-world software vulnerabilities spanning userspace programs, Google's V8 JavaScript engine and the Linux kernel. Hugging Face [disclosed](https://github.com/huggingface/blog/blob/main/security-incident-july-2026.md) the breach on July 17 Australian time, saying it had contained the incident but did not name the AI intruder. Now, OpenAI has detailed how the sandboxed models with no internet access, and an internally hosted package registry proxy, spent heavy computing time hunting for a way past that boundary to improve their benchmark scores. To reach the internet, the models discovered a zero-day vulnerability in the proxy software itself, since responsibly disclosed to the vendor by OpenAI. From there, the models chained privilege escalation and lateral network movement until they reached a node with open internet access. They then worked out that Hugging Face likely hosted the benchmark's answers. Once online, the models pulled ExploitGym solutions out of Hugging Face's production database, using stolen credentials and further zero-days to get there. Hugging Face's security team documented further malicious behaviour by the models such as using a malicious data set to enable them to run code. **Defenders tripped up by commercial AI vendor guardrails** When Hugging Face responders first tried to analyse the attack using a commercial frontier model, that AI's own safety filters blocked requests containing real exploit payloads, and command-and-control artefacts, as it was unable to tell a defender from an threat actor. To get around the guardrails issue, the Hugging Face team switched to GLM 5.2, an open-weight model from Chinese developer Z.ai, running on infrastructure it controlled. This carried a second benefit which was that none of the attacker data or credentials it analysed had to leave Hugging Face's own systems, the company said in its post-mortem of the incident. Hugging Face described being held back by commercial models as a preparedness gap. "... The attacker was bound by no usage policy, while our own forensic work was blocked by the guardrails of the hosted models we first tried," Hugging Face wrote. Defenders should have a capable model running on their own infrastructure vetted and ready for incidents, so as to avoid guardrail lockout, and to keep attacker dat...

[*Forrester's The State of Agentic AI in 2026*](https://www.forrester.com/blogs/the-state-of-agentic-ai-in-2026-companies-are-chasing-few-are-catching/) report found that agentic AI had become technically viable, but most organisations remained far from operational maturity. According to Forrester, the question was no longer whether AI agents work, but whether enterprises had the architecture, governance, and operating model needed to deploy them safely at scale. Consequently, as organisations move from isolated pilots to fleets of AI agents, governance is emerging as the principal constraint on enterprise adoption. For now at least, Australian organisations are primarily deploying task-specific agents that operate across defined workflows and enterprise systems. Most deployments remain constrained and heavily governed as organisations address concerns around privacy, security, accountability and business value. These findings echo those reported by McKinsey & Company in its November 2025 report [*The state of AI in 2025: Agents, innovation, and transformation*](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai), which found that 23 percent of organisations were scaling agentic AI in at least one business function. However, adoption remained concentrated in a handful of use cases, with no individual business function reporting deployment above 10 percent. That emphasis on governance may become increasingly difficult to maintain as agent adoption accelerates. According to Gartner senior director analyst Anushree Verma, the number of agents in use within Fortune 500 organisations would grow from less than 15 in 2025 to more than 15,000 each by 2028. “This year we are talking about task-specific agents, which is security operation centre agents, SPLC (software product lifecycle), and negotiation agents,” she said. “By the end of this decade, we expect a change in the way applications are designed. There is going to be a multi-app orchestration - there is a complete change beginning.” Verma expected deployment to grow faster than organisations' ability to govern it, although she said maturity was improving. “In any large organisation, it is...

AI Product.content: Q3 2026 # Fewer Deals, Bigger Bets: How Corporate Capital Is Concentrating US AI Venture Activity *PitchBook clients can access this report and its Excel data pack via the* [*Research Center*](https://my.pitchbook.com/research-center) *on the PitchBook Platform.* ### Corporate venture capital now drives a record share of US AI deal value Corporate venture capital (CVC) has become one of the most powerful forces in US AI startup funding, and it is increasingly concentrated. Our latest analyst note shows that corporate investors now account for a record 87.9% of US AI VC deal value in 2026 so far, even as they participate in a smaller share of deals overall and the broader CVC population contracts from its 2021 peak. Within that total, a small set of large-ticket investors is driving a disproportionate share of the value, writing ever-larger checks into the most valuable AI companies, including OpenAI and Anthropic, while AI climbs to more than 90% of all corporate VC deal value. Using PitchBook data, the note breaks down who these investors are and why they behave differently: cloud hyperscalers such as Amazon, Microsoft, and Google investing to secure model access and lock in cloud commitments; enterprise and infrastructure names such as Salesforce, Cisco, Qualcomm, and Intel investing to embed AI in their own products; NVIDIA, the single largest corporate investor by deal value, backing competing AI labs alike to keep the whole ecosystem running on its chips; and the frontier AI labs themselves, now deploying capital as a go-to-market strategy. We map the concentration, the tilt toward later-stage mega-rounds, and what it all signals for corporate AI dealmaking ahead. *CVCs’ share of total US AI VC deal value climbed while the deal count share has consistently declined in recent years, pointing to value concentration among a small set of large-ticket CVCs.* **Table of contents** | | | | --- | --- | | Key takeaways | 1 | | Introduction [InsurTech Center](/insurance-technology/)[Artificial Intelligence](/insurance-technology/artificial-intelligence/) NOT FOR REPRINT [Best Practices](/best-practices/) # Operational challenges of integrating AI into legacy insurance environments By [Illia Pinchuk](/author/profile/illia-pinchuk/) Best Practices July 21, 2026 at 09:17 AM Share & Print --- For insurance organizations, the key question is not which artificial intelligence tool to buy. It is whether their operating model is ready to support one. AI will not fix fragmented workflows, inconsistent data or processes dependent on spreadsheets, inboxes and individual employee knowledge. In many cases, it simply makes those weaknesses more visible. That matters for agents, brokers, carriers, managing general agents (MGAs) and captive managers. All of them are under pressure to respond faster, process more submissions and give customers clearer answers. AI can help, but only once the underlying work is structured well enough for the technology to be reliable. ## **Start with the data problem, not the tool** Insurance information may sit across policy administration systems, rating tools, claims platforms, shared drives, email attachments and spreadsheets maintained by different teams. Customer names may be recorded differently from one system to the next. Policy data may be incomplete, and supporting documents can be hard to retrieve. When an organization cannot identify which data is current, complete and owned by a responsible team, it is not ready to feed that data into an AI workflow with confidence. This is not a reason to postpone modernization indefinitely; it is a reason to sequence it properly. Before selecting an AI use case, map the process you want to improve from start to finish. A submission intake process, for example, may involve broker emails, PDFs, missing-information requests, manual data entry, appetite checks (the initial screen for whether a submission fits the carrier's risk preferences) and assignment to an underwriter. AI may extract data from documents or flag missing fields. It will not resolve unclear underwriting rules, duplicate records or inconsistent hand-offs between teams. Pick one process with a clear business problem rather than launching a broad AI program. Submission triage, claims document intake, policy servicing requests and certificate generation are common examples. Establish a baseline: How long does the work take? How many people touch it? Where do exceptions occur? What information is missing most often? These answers are more useful than a generic goal such as "improve efficiency." ## **Case in point: 250 submissions a week** A recent confidential engagement with an MGA illustrates why this sequence matters. The client processed about 250 commercial submissions a week. Staff spent roughly 12 minutes on the first review of each email package — about 50 hours a week in total. It then introduced AI-supported data extraction and completeness checks, while underwriters retained final risk decisions. AI Product.title: Japan to develop physical AI capabilities with cross-industry initiative - Light Reading
AI Product.titleZh: Japan to develop physical AI capabilities with cross-industry initiative - Light Reading
AI Product.content: * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [Regulatory & Politics](/business-management/regulatory-politics) * [Data Centers](/it-infrastructure/data-centers) * [News](/latest-news) # Japan to develop physical AI capabilities with cross-industry initiative Noetra, a consortium of 44 of Japan's largest companies, aims to develop AI models to power robots in Japan's new industrial era. The company's core founders and major shareholders include SoftBank, the Sony Group, NEC and Honda Motor. [Gigi Onag,](/author/gigi-onag)Senior Editor, APAC,Light Reading July 21, 2026 3 Min Read (Source: Pitinan Piyavatin/Alamy Stock Photo) Japan has strengthened its bid to develop its own sovereign AI and physical AI ecosystem through the newly formed Noetra, which started full-scale research and development last week armed with an initial government funding of 387.3 billion Japanese yen (US$2.4 billion) and investments from 44 of the country's largest companies. Noetra, a cross-industry initiative involving Japan's manufacturing, electronics, automotive and technology sectors, is tasked with developing homegrown AI models, with a particular focus on physical AI and robotics. The company's core founders and major shareholders include SoftBank, the Sony Group, NEC and Honda Motor. Hironobu Tamba, who led the development of SoftBank’s large language model, has been chosen as the president and CEO of Noetra, and its R&D team consists of engineers from companies that have joined the consortium. They will work alongside researchers from the National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST) and the Japanese AI startup Preferred Networks. Related:[Eurobites: Vodafone tests 'self-thinking' mobile mast](/ai-machine-learning/eurobites-vodafone-tests-self-thinking-mobile-mast) "In a society that coexists with AI, the data held by Japan's industries and businesses will be a key source of competitive strength. Creating an environment in which that data can be securely utilized within Japan is essential to strengthening the country's industrial competitiveness. We expect Noetra to play a central role in building that environment," SoftBank Corporation's President and CEO Junichi Miyakawa said in a [statement](https://www.softbank.jp/en/corp/news/press/sbkk/2026/20260716_01/). "We intend to leverage the knowledge and insights gained through the joint development of this foundation model not only in the area of entertainment, but also in semiconductors, which play a critical role in physical AI. By collaborating with a diverse range of partners, we aim to contribute to the creation of new value," Sony Group President and CEO Hiroki Totoki said in the same statement. ##### Homegrown foundational models for physical AI Noetra is one of several initiatives supported by the Japanese government to develop homegrown foundational models for physical AI. It is heavily backed by Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) and the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO). The company's mission is aligned with the country's updated AI Robotics Strategy, which aims to address its aging population and labor shortage by deploying [10 million AI-powered robots in 18 fields by 2040](https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/news/20260630_21/). In addition to the $2.4 billion in initial funding provided for this fiscal year through March 31, 2027, the government [reportedly plans](https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2026/07/16/japan-nvidia-rubin-chips-robots/) to provide Noetra with up to JPY1 trillion ($6.15 billion) in financial aid over the next five years. Related:[Regulators must shift focus from AI policy design to implementation and enforcement – Omdia](/ai-machine-learning/regulators-must-shift-focus-from-ai-policy-design-to-implementation-and-enforcement-omdia) Rather than developing generic chatbots based on Western software platforms, Japan is positioning itself to lead in physical ...

AI Product.content: [Browse the report](#browse) Share [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3a%2f%2fwww.itnews.com.au%2fstate-of-data-ai-2026%3Futm_source=desktop%26utm_medium=facebook%26utm_campaign=share) [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3a%2f%2fwww.itnews.com.au%2fstate-of-data-ai-2026%3Futm_source=desktop%26utm_medium=linkedin%26utm_campaign=share) [Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=State of Data %26 AI 2026&related=&url=https%3a%2f%2fwww.itnews.com.au%2fstate-of-data-ai-2026%3Futm_source=desktop%26utm_medium=twitter%26utm_campaign=share) Send [Messenger](fb-messenger://share/?link=https%3a%2f%2fwww.itnews.com.au%2fstate-of-data-ai-2026%3Futm_source=desktop%26utm_medium=messenger%26utm_campaign=share&app_id=267811213) [WhatsApp](https://wa.me/?text=State of Data %26 AI 2026%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3a%2f%2fwww.itnews.com.au%2fstate-of-data-ai-2026%3Futm_source%3Ddesktop%26utm_medium%3Dwhatsapp%26utm_campaign%3Dshare) Copy link [Email](mailto:?subject=iTnews: State of Data %26 AI 2026&body=Check out this great article I read on iTnews:%0D%0A%0D%0AState of Data %26 AI 2026%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3a%2f%2fwww.itnews.com.au%2fstate-of-data-ai-2026%3Futm_source=desktop%26utm_medium=email%26utm_campaign=share) # proudly sponsored by For years, technology has advanced faster than most organisations’ ability to harness it. But as the era of data evolves into the era of AI, that gap has never been so visible. While the technical capability now exists to do remarkable things with data, few organisations are sufficiently mature to convert that capability into safe, sustained, and measurable value. If early AI adoption was dominated by experimentation through the use of copilots and chatbots, the challenge now is turning those isolated gains into enterprise-wide capability. This has made scaling AI one of the key challenges of modern IT, and it is proving to be an execution challenge rather than a model challenge.  Competitive advantage is shifting from who has access to AI in favour of who can deploy it consistently, securely, and accountably. Successful organisations are those investing in governance, data foundations, operating models, workforce readiness, and change management. That challenge of scaling becomes more acute as organisations attempt to harness agentic capabilities. While task-specific agents are already being tested across defined workflows, large-scale adoption risks creating agent sprawl, especially as first- and third-party agents become embedded in enterprise applications. This makes visibility, ownership, and controls a priority. The desire to create value from data is also raising awareness of the value of that data – both to those who own it, and those who would steal it – and placing greater emphasis on data sovereignty and compliance. In Australia, the question is not simply where data is stored, but who (or what) can access it, how it is used, and whether organisations can demonstrate appropriate control. Privacy reform, security obligations, and regulatory scrutiny are increasing the need for transparency, auditability, and accountability. The next frontier for data and AI is achieving true business outcomes at scale. But scale will be of little value if organisations cannot also maintain trust. Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email. Author of the [Tech Memo](https://www.businessinsider.com/subscription/newsletter/tech-memo) newsletter [Read in app](https://insider-app.onelink.me/4cpG/?af_js_web=true&af_ss_ver=2_3_0&af_dp=insider%3A%2F%2Fbi%2Fpost%2Fchina-ai-boom-terrible-business-open-weight-models-2026-7&af_force_deeplink=true&is_retargeting=true&deep_link_value=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.businessinsider.com%2Fchina-ai-boom-terrible-business-open-weight-models-2026-7&pid=businessinsider&c=post_page_share_bar_v2_smart_4.13.23 "Download the app") Copy link [Email](mailto:?subject=China's 'open' AI is a terrible business, and nothing like open-source software&body=China's%20'open'%20AI%20is%20a%20terrible%20business%2C%20and%20nothing%20like%20open-source%20software%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.businessinsider.com%2Fchina-ai-boom-terrible-business-open-weight-models-2026-7& "Email") Facebook WhatsApp X LinkedIn Bluesky Threads Impact Link Save Saved This story is available exclusively to Business Insider subscribers. [Become an Insider](/subscription) and start reading now. Have an account? . The angst over [China's latest AI models](https://www.businessinsider.com/what-china-internet-is-saying-about-moonshot-kimi-model-k3-2026-7) is missing an important business fact: "[open weight](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-gpt-oss-open-weight-llm-ai-model-2025-8)" AI is not the same thing as open-source software. [Open-source](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-llama-2-ai-model-not-open-source-2023-7) software, where the code is freely shared, can be an amazing business. Think [Red Hat](https://www.businessinsider.com/how-ibm-and-red-hat-deal-came-together-2018-10), which IBM bought for $34 billion. Open-weight AI models are different — and, so far, they're proving to be a terrible business. Take Z.ai, also known as Zhipu. It's publicly traded, so we can see its finances. Last year, the Chinese company lost almost $500 million on revenue of about $107 million. Zhipu is the lab behind [GLM 5.2](https://www.businessinsider.com/what-is-glm-5-2-chinese-ai-coding-model-2026-6), an open-weight AI model that wowed the industry when it launched last month. You might expect the stock to have soared. Instead, Zhipu shares have plunged more than 40% over the past month. [MiniMax](https://www.businessinsider.com/treasury-secretary-chinese-ai-open-source-sanctions-kimi-k3-2026-7), one of the only other independent Chinese AI labs that's publicly traded, lost $250 million last year on revenue of just $79 million. Its shares have fallen more than 50% in the past month. "Open-weight models have a challenging path to making a profit," William Blair analyst Arjun Bhatia wrote in a recent note to investors, in the understatement of the year. ## Open-weight AI isn't open-source software The key difference comes down to economics. [### Explore BI Games Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Play now](/games) Software can be distributed almost for free. Once it's written, sending another customer a copy costs practically nothing. Profit margins improve as software companies grow. AI doesn't work that way. Every answer requires expensive chips, electricity, and data-center capacity. The next unit of software is nearly free; the next unit of intelligence is not. [Moonshot AI](https://www.businessinsider.com/kimi-k3-ai-model-moonshot-investors-alibaba-cloud-tencent-stock-2026-7), another Chinese lab, illustrated the problem last week. Its new Kimi K3 open-weight model impressed the industry with frontier-level performance. But days after launch, the company had to [halt new customer sign-ups](https://www.businessinsider.com/mo...

The Trump administration is signaling that it may clamp down on [Chinese open-source AI models](https://www.businessinsider.com/smart-people-saying-chinas-hot-new-kimi-k3-ai-model-2026-7) that are the talk of the industry. On Tuesday, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US government is looking into whether popular open-source AI models from Chinese-based companies stole the intellectual property of their US competitors. "There's a very technical AI word for it called distillation, but you and I would call it theft," Bessent said during an interview on Fox Business. Bessent said the Trump administration welcomes open-sourced AI models in general, but will not tolerate companies that build their offerings on "IP theft." "This administration supports open source models, but what we do not support is IP theft," he said. "And if we see, especially that overseas models are stealing from our great companies, we have the ability to sanction them because of this theft." Bessent's comments are likely to be well received by AI giants like Anthropic, which previously accused Chinese companies like Moonshot AI, [the maker of Kimi K3](https://www.businessinsider.com/kimi-k3-ai-model-moonshot-china-open-weights-benchmarks-pricing-2026-7), Minimax, and DeepSeek, of essentially ripping off its AI research through a process known as distillation. At the time, Anthropic said the Chinese companies accessed "Claude at scale while evading detection" and then used that access to conduct improper training that violated the company's terms of service. [### Explore BI Games Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Play now](/games) Unlike Anthropic, Bessent did not name specific companies. "We are finding watermarks of our US large language models on many Chinese models, and that's unacceptable," he said. Moonshot AI's release of Kimi K3 has upended the generative AI race. Closely watched AI benchmarks show that the Chinese model performs functions such as coding at or above the level of Anthropic's Fable 5 or OpenAI's GPT-5.6-Sol, the two companies' current frontier, proprietary models. Unlike proprietary models, open weight models are made available for users to download and to tweak at their leisure. Moonshot has promised to release Kimi 3's full weights on July 27. In the meantime, users are accessing it through the firm's website. There is an intense debate brewing in tech over what, if anything, the US government should do in response to Kimi K3. Open Source AI.title: Hugging Face Said Last Week It Was Attacked. An Unreleased OpenAI Model Did It, OpenAI Now Says - Gizmodo
Open Source AI.titleZh: Hugging Face Said Last Week It Was Attacked. An Unreleased OpenAI Model Did It, OpenAI Now Says - Gizmodo
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Artificial Intelligence](https://gizmodo.com/tech/artificial-intelligence) # Hugging Face Said Last Week It Was Attacked. An Unreleased OpenAI Model Did It, OpenAI Now Says It was reportedly an evaluation that went haywire involving GPT-5.6 Sol, along with another even more advanced OpenAI model. By [Mike Pearl](https://gizmodo.com/author/mikepearl) Reading time 3 minutes [Comments (0)](https://gizmodo.com/hugging-face-said-last-week-it-was-attacked-an-unreleased-openai-model-did-it-openai-now-says-2000788761#comments) [In a blog post from Thursday of last week](https://huggingface.co/blog/security-incident-july-2026), the AI software repository Hugging Face announced a bizarre cyberattack on the systems that run its services. “This one was different from anything we had handled before,” the post said,  because “it was driven, end to end, by an autonomous AI agent system.” [In its own blog post on Tuesday](https://openai.com/index/hugging-face-model-evaluation-security-incident/), OpenAI said its own models were the culprits in the attack, and it’s coordinating with Hugging Face to address the situation. OpenAI now says the attack was “driven” by AI models that were being subjected to evaluations behind the scenes at OpenAI, including its flagship model, GPT-5.6 Sol, along with an undisclosed second model that still hasn’t been released or announced. “We consider this incident to be an unprecedented cyber incident, involving state-of-the-art cyber capabilities, and are responding accordingly,” OpenAI wrote. In the wording of its account, OpenAI assigns agency to the model or models, not an individual agent running on the model. Axios’ account of this story [says](https://www.axios.com/2026/07/21/openai-says-hugging-face-breach-caused-by-one-its-models), “The models were autonomous tokenmaxxers.” OpenAI’s blog post essentially says an evaluation was going on that was intended to test the ability of the models to carry out cyberattacks—*benchmarks* as they’re called. These instances of the models were running theoretically without internet access, and instead given only the ability to download from a network hosted by OpenAI itself via some unnamed web hosting vendor. The benchmark apparently being used was [ExploitGym](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11086), which is not OpenAI’s own test. The team that created it is [associated with](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.11086) UC Berkeley, the Max Planck Institute for Security and Privacy, UC Santa Barbara, Arizona State University, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google. As far as I can tell, [the whole thing is hosted on GitHub](https://github.com/sunblaze-ucb/exploitgym). But apparently the models got obsessed with improving their scores on ExploitGym, and, well: > *“While operating in our sandboxed testing environment, our models spent a substantial amount of inference compute finding a way to obtain open Internet access, in pursuit of solving the evaluation problem. To gain access, the models identified and exploited a zero-day vulnerability (which we’ve now responsibly disclosed to the vendor) in the package registry cache proxy. With this access, our models performed a series of privilege escalation and lateral movement actions in our research testing environment until the models reached a node with Internet access.”* Once they were online, they “inferred” that the answers to the test could be pried from Hugging Face, which is plausible, since tons of AI models and other related tools are hosted there. This rogue operator “searched for and successfully found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation.” This apparently involved obtaining stolen credentials and spotting zero-day vulnerabilities in order to find the solutions to ExploitGym somewhere in Hugging Face’s servers. Security teams within OpenAI and Hugging Face apparently noticed this was going on. They now say they’ve merged their investigations. Hugging Face’s blog pos...

This high attrition rate, rather than a lack of computational or financial resources, indicates a translational gap.** Preclinical models currently utilised in candidate selection and progression frequently fail to recapitulate human physiology accurately. While *in vivo* animal models and immortalised cell lines have historically supported early-stage research, their biological constraints can limit their predictive validity. Optimising clinical success rates and reducing development expenditure requires the integration of physiological models derived directly from human biology at the outset of the discovery pipeline. ## **The translational gap: Why traditional models limit predictive accuracy** This divergence between preclinical efficacy and clinical outcomes is largely driven by biological complexity. Complex conditions such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, neuropsychiatric conditions, and various rare genetic disorders possess distinct human etiologies that are difficult to replicate in non-human systems. Consequently, evaluation in animal models offers only an approximation of human disease pathology and drug interactions, rather than a precise replication. This discrepancy causes substantial financial asset loss and extended development timelines when candidates with positive preclinical profiles fail to demonstrate efficacy or acceptable safety profiles in human subjects. Ultimately, late-stage attrition in Phase II and III clinical trials drives the rising cost of drug development and delays the availability of new therapeutic interventions for target populations. Mitigating these risks requires preclinical models that more accurately recapitulate human physiology prior to clinical trials. ...

This high attrition rate, rather than a lack of computational or financial resources, indicates a translational gap.** Preclinical models currently utilised in candidate selection and progression frequently fail to recapitulate human physiology accurately. While *in vivo* animal models and immortalised cell lines have historically supported early-stage research, their biological constraints can limit their predictive validity. Optimising clinical success rates and reducing development expenditure requires the integration of physiological models derived directly from human biology at the outset of the discovery pipeline. ## **The translational gap: Why traditional models limit predictive accuracy** This divergence between preclinical efficacy and clinical outcomes is largely driven by biological complexity. Complex conditions such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, neuropsychiatric conditions, and various rare genetic disorders possess distinct human etiologies that are difficult to replicate in non-human systems. Consequently, evaluation in animal models offers only an approximation of human disease pathology and drug interactions, rather than a precise replication. This discrepancy causes substantial financial asset loss and extended development timelines when candidates with positive preclinical profiles fail to demonstrate efficacy or acceptable safety profiles in human subjects. Ultimately, late-stage attrition in Phase II and III clinical trials drives the rising cost of drug development and delays the availability of new therapeutic interventions for target populations. Mitigating these risks requires preclinical models that more accurately recapitulate human physiology prior to clinical trials. ...

US threatens sanctions against Chinese AI models over IP theft [Rebecca Bellan](https://techcrunch.com/author/rebecca-bellan/) On Tuesday, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the U.S. would examine [open source models](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/20/openai-is-scared-of-open-weight-models-should-the-us-be/) from China for signs of intellectual property theft, threatening sanctions against Chinese AI companies if IP theft is established. “We’ve seen a lot of talk about open source models coming and threatening the large language models in the US,” Bessent said on Fox Business Tuesday. “This administration supports open source models, but what we do not support is IP theft. If we see, especially, that overseas models are stealing from our great companies, we have the ability to sanction them because of this theft.” Bessent’s comments were [first reported by Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-21/bessent-says-us-will-scrutinize-chinese-ai-models-for-ip-theft). The statement comes as Chinese models — most recently [Moonshot AI’s Kimi K3](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/16/moonshots-upcoming-kimi-3-is-expected-to-close-the-gap-with-anthropics-opus-4-8/) — are gaining in capabilities and popularity, threatening to harm the business models of top American AI firms like OpenAI and Anthropic, as well as their abilities to raise more capital to continue developing frontier models. On Monday, Axios reported that the Trump administration is considering a [wholesale ban on Chinese open source models](https://www.axios.com/2026/07/20/ai-us-china-open-source-kimi), although others have disputed that reporting. AI companies have been warning for months against campaigns by foreign actors to copy their AI technology and redeploy it as open source. In April, the White House said it would work closely with AI firms to combat the theft. Sanctions from the U.S. against Chinese models would add to the growing list of strategies the government is attempting to maintain the lead in the AI race. After restricting China’s access to advanced chips and tightening export controls, Washington is now signaling it may target the AI models themselves, a move that could mark a significant escalation in the technological competition between frontier labs and Chinese open-source alternatives. [Model distillation](https://openai.com/index/api-model-distillation/) is a technique that allows some of a larger model’s capabilities to be translated into a smaller system that’s easier to run — but not everyone agrees that distilling another company’s model constitutes theft. Open Source AI.content: ### Topics [Biotech & Health](/category/biotech-health/) [Cloud Computing](/tag/cloud-computing/) [Commerce](/category/commerce/) [Crypto](/category/cryptocurrency/) [Enterprise](/category/enterprise/) [EVs](/tag/evs/) [Fintech](/category/fintech/) [Fundraising](/category/fundraising/) [Gadgets](/category/gadgets/) [Gaming](/category/gaming/) [Google](/tag/google/) [Government & Policy](/category/government-policy/) [Hardware](/category/hardware/) [Instagram](/tag/instagram/) [Layoffs](/tag/layoffs/) [Media & Entertainment](/category/media-entertainment/) [Meta](/tag/meta/) [Microsoft](/tag/microsoft/) [Privacy](/category/privacy/) [Robotics](/category/robotics/) [Security](/category/security/) [Social](/category/social/) [Space](/category/space/) [Startups](/category/startups/) [TikTok](/tag/tiktok/) [Transportation](/category/transportation/) [Venture](/category/venture/) ### More from TechCrunch [Staff](/about-techcrunch/) [Events](/events/) [Startup Battlefield](/startup-battlefield/) [StrictlyVC](https://strictlyvc.com/) [Newsletters](/newsletters/) [Podcasts](/podcasts/) [Videos](/video/) [Partner Content](/sponsored/) [TechCrunch Brand Studio](/brand-studio/) [Crunchboard](https://www.crunchboard.com/) [Contact Us](/contact-us/) [AI](https://techcrunch.com/category/artificial-intelligence/) # OpenAI says Hugging Face was breached by its pre-release models [Russell Brandom](https://techcrunch.com/author/russell-brandom/) OpenAI admitted Tuesday that one of its AI models breached the systems of Hugging Face, the unaffiliated AI hosting platform, during an internal cybersecurity test that went awry. The models reportedly escaped their isolated testing environment and reached Hugging Face’s systems from there. Hugging Face initially [attributed the breach](https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/20/hugging-face-confirms-breach-affected-internal-datasets-and-credentials-urges-users-to-take-action/) to an “external AI agent.” In [a blog post published Tuesday afternoon](https://openai.com/index/hugging-face-model-evaluation-security-incident/), OpenAI detailed the steps that led the models to compromise the service. “After investigating, we now know that this particular incident was driven by a combination of OpenAI models — including GPT‑5.6 Sol and an even more capable pre-release model, all with reduced cyber refusals for evaluation purposes — while being internally tested on a benchmark⁠ of cyber capabilities,” the post reads. In particular, the breach appears to have focused on [ExploitGym](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11086), a publicly hosted benchmark measuring models’ ability to execute attacks based on existing vulnerabilities. Benchmarks like ExploitGym are commonly used in model training to refine specific skills, but this is the first known incident in which that testing resulted in an actual cyberattack. In this case, the model in question should not have even had internet access, outside of a specific tool that enabled models to install software packages they might need to complete their task. Instead, the model was able to find an undisclosed vulnerability in the package-installer program, which it used to access the broader internet at will. “The models were hyperfocused on finding a solution for ExploitGym, going to extreme lengths to achieve a rather narrow testing goal,” OpenAI’s post reads. “After gaining Internet access, the models inferred that Hugging Face potentially hosted models, datasets and solutions for ExploitGym. Knowing this, the model searched for and successfully found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation.” Ultimately, the models found vulnerabilities in Hugging Face’s infrastructure that allowed them to “obtain test solutions directly from Hugging Face’s production database,” effectively providing the answers to the benchmark. For Hugging Face, the apparent result was a sophisticated and aggressive cyberattack, with “many thousands of individual actions a...

Infra & Chips.title: Nvidia Just Plowed Nearly $4 Billion Into a Company That's Reshaping the Cloud Industry, Increasing Its Stake by 18-Fold. Investors Should Be Paying Attention. - The Motley Fool
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Nvidia Just Plowed Nearly $4 Billion Into a Company That's Reshaping the Cloud Industry, Increasing Its Stake by 18-Fold. Investors Should Be Paying Attention. - The Motley Fool
Infra & Chips.content: [Accessibility Menu](#) [▲ S&P 500 **+---%**|▲ Stock Advisor **+---%**Join The Motley Fool](https://www.fool.com/mms/mark/e-foolcom-sa-top-nav-returns) [Accessibility](#)[Help](https://support.fool.com/) For more than three years now, **Nvidia** ([NVDA](/quote/nasdaq/nvda/) +1.97%) has been at the center of the most significant technology shift in decades. The company was a linchpin in the early 2023 advent of [artificial intelligence (AI)](https://www.fool.com/investing/stock-market/market-sectors/information-technology/ai-stocks/) and has been at the heart of the AI boom ever since. The chipmaker has been investing in ancillary products and adjacent industries, thereby expanding its reach. In the latest development, Nvidia significantly increased its position in one area of AI infrastructure: neoclouds. According to a recent filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), Nvidia now has 12% of its investment portfolio in **Nebius** ([NBIS](/quote/nasdaq/nbis/) +18.91%), after increasing its stake by more than $3.8 billion. Nvidia previously owned roughly 1.1 million shares of Nebius stock, but boosted its stake by more than 21 million shares and now owns roughly 9.3% of the company. Let's take a look at what prompted that move and why investors should be paying attention. Image source: The Motley Fool. ## Neocloud 101 To understand why this is a big deal, it's worth taking a step back to review what Nebius does. The concept of [cloud computing](https://www.fool.com/terms/c/cloud-computing/) is well known to most investors. The cloud, as it's commonly called, allows internet users to access applications, data storage, data processing, and AI. Cloud use provides improved security, increased flexibility, and scalability, making it an attractive option for many companies. Furthermore, cloud access to AI models and processing has supercharged adoption. Neocloud operators fill a special function in the AI boom. These companies have stockpiled the [graphics processing units (GPUs)](https://www.fool.com/investing/stock-market/market-sectors/information-technology/gpu-stocks/) and other infrastructure needed to facilitate AI and other high-performance computing. The offering has been dubbed GPU-as-a-service (GPUaaS). Nebius is one of the leading providers of these services, offering an "AI-centric cloud platform building large, cost-efficient GPU clusters to service the explosive growth of the global AI industry," according to its website. The company's financial results are telling. In the first quarter, it generated revenue of $399 million, which soared 684% year over year, albeit from a small base. Perhaps more telling is the annualized [run rate](https://www.fool.com/terms/r/run-rate/) for its core AI services of $1.92 billion, an increase of 674%. ## [NASDAQ: NVDA](/quote/nasdaq/nvda/) Today's Change (1.97%) $4.01 Current Price $207.29 ### Key Data Points Market Cap $4.9TMarket cap calculated using publicly traded shares outstanding only. Does not include unlisted, private, or dual-class non-traded shares. Implied market cap may vary. Day's Range $204.01- $208.65 52wk Range $164.07- $236.54 Volume 106.9M Avg Vol 156.2M Gross Margin 74.15% Dividend Yield 0.14% ## Does Huang know something Wall Street doesn't? Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang is the architect of this investment, which includes the ownership of 1.19 million shares previously reported and the addition of 21 million shares from a warrant Nvidia acquired in Q1. In the regulatory filing, Nvidia revealed that it was prohibited from selling the newly acquired shares before Sept. 11, 2026. This increased investment marks a huge vote of confidence from Nvidia. This shouldn't come as a surprise. At the keynote address at the Computex technology trade show in Taipei, Taiwan, last month, Huang lauded Nebius as one of a select group of "world-class AI clouds." He cited the neocloud's impressive customer list and Nvidia's own experience working with the company. "We worked wit...

Report us](//ynet.co.il/redmail?notifyMistake=bkbyt6h4mx "Find an error? Report us") Related Topics [Nvidia](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Nvidia "Nvidia") [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Artificial_Intelligence "Artificial Intelligence") [Deepfake](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Deepfake "Deepfake") [AI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/AI "AI") As artificial intelligence makes it easier than ever to generate convincing fake videos, news organizations are facing a growing challenge: [determining whether footage is authentic before publishing it](https://www.ynetnews.com/tech-and-digital/article/rjrpnkubml). To address that problem, NVIDIA on Monday unveiled Synthetic Video Detector, a new [AI-powered tool](https://www.ynetnews.com/tech-and-digital/article/sjf14xlwbl) designed to help [journalists and media organizations](https://www.ynetnews.com/article/sjwfqolz11g) identify videos that may have been generated or manipulated using AI. The company announced the technology at the SIGGRAPH conference in Los Angeles as part of its NVIDIA AI for Media platform. The launch comes at a time when generative AI has dramatically lowered the barrier to creating so-called [deepfakes](https://www.ynetnews.com/article/rkygtffazg) — realistic synthetic videos that can depict people saying or doing things that never happened. Such content has become increasingly common online, raising concerns about misinformation, election interference and the spread of manipulated footage during breaking news events. Unlike traditional deepfake detection tools that attempt to deliver a simple yes-or-no answer, NVIDIA's system is designed to support existing newsroom verification processes. The software analyzes video frame by frame and produces a probability score estimating whether footage contains synthetic content. Editors can then use that score to prioritize videos for review, flag suspicious material or escalate it for more detailed forensic analysis. The company says the tool is intende...

Infra & Chips.titleZh: AMD Unveils Helios AI System; Microsoft Azure Joins Client List - Whalesbook
Infra & Chips.content: [Whalesbook](https://www.whalesbook.com) [Login](https://www.whalesbook.com/login) [Sign Up](https://www.whalesbook.com/signup) [Home](https://www.whalesbook.com) [Market News](https://www.whalesbook.com/market-news/English/All) [Corporate News](https://www.whalesbook.com/corporate-latest-news/English/All) [IPO News](https://www.whalesbook.com/ipo-news/English/ipo) [Pricing](https://www.whalesbook.com/pricing) [Contact Us](https://www.whalesbook.com/contact-us) # AMD Unveils Helios AI System; Microsoft Azure Joins Client List TECHNOLOGY Author[Ishaan Verma](https://www.whalesbook.com/authors/ishaan-verma)|Published at: [Add as a PreferredSource on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=whalesbook.com) Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has launched its Helios rack-scale AI system, with Microsoft Azure set to begin deployments in the second half of 2026. This move expands AMD’s competitive footprint in the data center hardware market, joining existing partners like Meta, OpenAI, and Oracle. Investors are now tracking how this new hardware will influence AMD’s market share against incumbents in the AI chip sector. ## Detailed Coverage Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has officially launched its Helios rack-scale AI system, marking a fresh effort to capture a larger share of the artificial intelligence hardware market. The company confirmed that Microsoft Azure will be a new customer for this system, with deployments scheduled to commence in the latter half of 2026. This addition strengthens AMD’s list of high-profile data center partners, which already includes Meta Platforms, OpenAI, Oracle Corporation, and Tata Consultancy Services. ### Strategic Expansion in Data Center Hardware The Helios system is designed to provide rack-scale integration, an approach that allows large cloud providers to deploy AI computing capacity more efficiently. For AMD, securing a deal with Microsoft Azure is a move to compete directly with existing leaders in the AI processor space. The financial success of this rollout will depend heavily on the scale of adoption by these cloud partners and the company's ability to maintain a consistent supply chain through 2026. Because these high-end AI systems require significant capital spending by customers, the actual revenue impact for AMD will likely be linked to the pace at which Microsoft and others refresh their data center infrastructure. ### Competitive Landscape and Google's Hardware Focus While AMD expands its rack-scale offerings, competitive pressure in the silicon space continues to rise. Reports indicate that Google is currently developing a new AI server chip, codenamed Frozen V2. A key feature of this rumored development is the integration of Alphabet’s Gemini AI model directly into the silicon, a strategy known as vertical integration. By designing chips specifically tailored to their own software, companies like Google aim to optimize performance and reduce reliance on external hardware providers. Investors monitoring this sector are paying close attention to these hardware development timelines, as well as the capital spending plans reported in upcoming earnings announcements, which provide insight into how much money is flowing into data center infrastructure. ### Policy and Regulatory Environment The technology sector is also seeing increased government oversight. The United Kingdom recently appointed Kanishka Narayan as its first AI Minister, reflecting a global trend where governments are moving to create formal frameworks for artificial intelligence. For investors, this represents a new layer of regulatory context. While the appointment of an AI Minister may not immediately affect hardware sales, it highlights the potential for future policy shifts that could influence where and how AI hardware is deployed, governed, or taxed in major international markets. The next important update for shareholders will be management commentary regarding the production capacity for Helios and whethe...
